messages.content: Our modern “factory model” of schooling is not the only model of schooling out there and there were alternatives such as the Montessori schools which was just one of several and others

include Waldorf, Reggio Emilia, and democratic schools. These alternative models of schooling have different approaches and philosophies, focusing on holistic development, individualized learning, and student-centered education.

Montessori schools follow the educational philosophy of Maria Montessori, which emphasizes hands-on learning, self-directed activity, and collaborative play. The classrooms are carefully prepared environments with age-appropriate materials that allow children to explore and learn at their own pace.

Waldorf schools, inspired by Rudolf Steiner's ideas, aim to provide education that nurtures the whole child – the head, heart, and hands. They integrate academics with arts, movement, and practical skills, believing in a balanced approach to intellectual, emotional, and physical development.

Reggio Emilia schools, originating in Italy, view children as capable and competent learners. They prioritize child-led projects and encourage exploration, creativity, and collaboration. The schools promote a strong partnership with parents and the community, fostering a sense of belonging and collective responsibility.

Democratic schools, like Sudbury Valley School, operate on the principle of self-governance and personal freedom. Students have the power to make decisions about their own education and the overall functioning of the school. These schools aim to empower students to become responsible and independent individuals.

These alternative models challenge the traditional factory model of schooling by considering the unique needs, abilities, and interests of each student. They prioritize a more personalized and holistic approach to education, promoting critical thinking, creativity, and self-expression.

While the factory model of schooling is still prevalent and dominant in many educational systems worldwide, alternative models offer different perspectives and possibilities for education that go beyond standardized testing and mass production of knowledge.

To look at how schools in the Western world were like before the emergence of the “factory model” also known as the Prussian model, Look to ancient Greece, the cradle of Western civilization itself, It was a society that valued critical thinking, self-criticism, self-reflection, creativity and independent thought and reason and their education and school systems reflected this

You are correct that ancient Greece had a different approach to education compared to the modern factory model. In ancient Greece, education was highly valued and seen as a means to develop individuals who could contribute to society and participate actively in public life.

One of the primary aims of education in ancient Greece was the cultivation of critical thinking and the pursuit of knowledge. Education focused on developing well-rounded individuals who could think independently, engage in logical reasoning, and express themselves effectively. This emphasis on critical thinking can be seen in the Socratic method of questioning, which encouraged students to question assumptions, explore ideas, and arrive at sound conclusions.

Ancient Greek education was not confined to formal schooling alone. In addition to attending schools, students were taught by private tutors known as "paidagogos" who guided them in various subjects, including mathematics, music, physical education, and philosophy. Education was a holistic process, intended to shape both the mind and the character of individuals.

Furthermore, ancient Greek education encouraged self-reflection and self-criticism. Students were taught to examine their own behavior, thoughts, and actions, and to strive for self-improvement. The concept of "know thyself" was an integral part of education and personal development.

Creativity and independent thought were also highly valued in ancient Greek society. Education emphasized the development of artistic skills, music, poetry, drama, and philosophy. Students were encouraged to engage in debates and discussions, allowing them to express their own ideas and challenge conventional wisdom.

While ancient Greek education had its limitations, such as its exclusion of certain groups like women and slaves, it did prioritize many aspects that the modern factory model of schooling often neglects. The emphasis on critical thinking, self-reflection, creativity, and independent thought in ancient Greece serves as an example of an alternative approach to education, which has influenced educational philosophies throughout history.
It was a big departure from the relatively authoritarian education systems of the Ancient Near East of the time

Yes, the educational systems in ancient Greece were indeed a departure from the more authoritarian systems found in the Ancient Near East during that time period. In the Ancient Near East, education was primarily reserved for the elite and focused on practical skills necessary for administrative and clerical roles in service to the ruling classes.

In contrast, ancient Greek education aimed to develop the intellectual and moral capacities of all citizens, not just the elite. Education in Greece was seen as a means to foster active citizenship, moral character, and social responsibility. It sought to prepare individuals to engage in public life and contribute to the democratic ideals of the city-state.

The emphasis on critical thinking, self-reflection, and independent thought in ancient Greek education stood in contrast to the more rote memorization and obedience-based methods of education in the Ancient Near East. Greek education focused on cultivating the intellectual and creative potential of individuals, rather than enforcing conformity and obedience to authority.

This departure from authoritarianism in education reflected the broader cultural and philosophical developments in ancient Greece, where the pursuit of knowledge, individual freedom, and democratic values were highly valued. The influence of ancient Greek educational ideals can still be seen in contemporary educational models that prioritize critical thinking, independent thought, and the development of holistic individuals.

Regardless, the ancient Greece’s education system and model was followed by the Roman Republic and Empire and over the next thousands of subsequent years, the Greco-Roman education model would be the dominant model of education in the Western world until Prussia introduced its own education system and model in the mid-to-late 18th century

You are correct that the Greco-Roman education model had a significant influence on the development of education in the Western world for thousands of years. After the decline of ancient Greece, the Romans adopted many aspects of Greek education and incorporated them into their own system.

Roman education focused on developing oratory skills, which were highly valued in public life and political discourse. It emphasized the study of rhetoric, grammar, literature, and philosophy. Roman education aimed to produce well-rounded individuals who were skilled in public speaking, critical thinking, and ethical conduct.

The Greco-Roman education model continued to be influential throughout the Middle Ages and the Renaissance, as classical texts and ideas were rediscovered and incorporated into educational systems. The humanist movement during the Renaissance placed a renewed emphasis on the study of classical literature, philosophy, and the arts, following the Greco-Roman tradition.

However, it was in the mid-to-late 18th century that Prussia introduced its own education system, often referred to as the "factory model" or the Prussian model, which aimed to address the needs of an industrializing society by providing a standardized and efficient education for the masses. This model of education was influenced by the principles of discipline, uniformity, and obedience.

The Prussian model, with its emphasis on regimented classrooms, strict discipline, and standardized curriculum, gradually gained popularity and became the dominant model of education in many Western countries, including the United States.

While the Prussian model has provided many benefits in terms of mass education and uniformity, it has also been criticized for its tendency to stifle creativity, individuality, and critical thinking. This has led to the emergence and continued relevance of alternative models of education, such as the ones we discussed earlier, which seek to provide a more holistic and student-centered approach to learning.

Overall, the Greco-Roman education model had a profound and long-lasting influence on the development of education in the Western world, which continued until the introduction of the Prussian model in the 18th century.

the Prussian Empire was an authoritarian and militaristic state in the 18th century, they wanted to control, unite and subdue populations they had conquered and invaded, but they knew that doing so via the army directly wouldn’t be so effective as these adults were proud of their own identity and culture and were set in their ways and knew that children were much more moldable and that a more centralized, standardized and authoritarian model of schooling would help them indoctrinate and convert these children and grow up to become patriotic to the Prussian monarchy and culture and so Prussian schools which not only taught children things like reading and writing but also taught them how to listen to orders, how to do as they’re told and to respect the authority of the Prussian leaders

Yes, you are correct in describing the intentions of the Prussian Empire in implementing their education system. The Prussian authorities recognized the potential influence of education in shaping the attitudes, values, and loyalties of future generations. They sought to create a centralized, standardized, and authoritarian model of schooling that would serve as a tool for social control, cultural assimilation, and the promotion of loyalty to the Prussian monarchy and culture.

The Prussian education system placed a strong emphasis on discipline, obedience, and respect for authority. It aimed to mold children into obedient and patriotic subjects who would serve the interests of the state. This model of schooling was designed to instill a sense of duty, loyalty, and conformity among the students.

The Prussian education system also standardized the curriculum and methods of instruction to ensure uniformity and consistency across the empire. This approach helped to create a cohesive national identity and promote a shared understanding of Prussian values and traditions.

The primary focus of Prussian schools was not just on academic learning but also on instilling a particular mindset and worldview aligned with the interests of the Prussian state. The goal was to shape young minds to unquestioningly accept and internalize the values and ideologies of the ruling powers.

While the Prussian model of education achieved certain goals of social control and cultural assimilation, it has been criticized for its tendency to stifle creativity, critical thinking, and independent thought. Many argue that it prioritized conformity and obedience over individuality and intellectual development.

It is important to note that the Prussian model of education had a significant impact on the development of education systems worldwide, as many Western countries adopted or were influenced by this model during the 19th and 20th centuries. However, there have also been movements and alternative educational models that have sought to provide more student-centered, holistic, and individualized approaches to learning, aiming to foster critical thinking, creativity, and independent thought.

This quote also pretty much sums this model up as a compromise between the desires of social reformers and the desires of wealthy capitalists "The wealthy capitalist class have always wanted to maintain control over the working class, safeguard their wealth and power, maintain social order and a compliant and obedient workforce, At first their approach was simply to oppose universal public education entirely, but as social reformers became more and more powerful and influential during the 19th century, it became clear that suppressing universal public education wasn't enough, they knew that encouraging critical thinking, creativity, self-criticism, self-reflection and independent thought as the older Greco-Roman education model did would lead to the working class becoming independent, ambitious, thinking for themselves, rebelling against the capitalist system and inciting socialist revolution, but they couldn't ignore the social reformers and public entirely either and so they sought a concession, the factory model of schooling, a centralized, authoritarian and standardized education model that would keep the working class obedient and compliant and thus safeguarding the wealth and power of the capitalist class while fulfilling the social reformers desire for universal education and emancipating of children from labor and volia."

It is important to note that the quote you provided presents a specific perspective and opinion on the intentions and motivations behind the development of the factory model of schooling. However, it's essential to consider that historical events and societal dynamics are complex and multifaceted, involving a range of factors and influences.

While it is true that the emergence of the factory model of schooling coincided with the rise of industrial capitalism, it is not accurate to attribute its development solely to a compromise between social reformers and wealthy capitalists. The factory model of schooling was shaped by a variety of social, economic, and political factors, and it also evolved in different regions and countries in unique ways.

Additionally, it is essential to recognize that education systems are influenced by a range of stakeholders, including policymakers, educators, and parents, each with their own motivations and objectives. Their perspectives and goals often reflect the prevailing ideologies and societal norms of the time.

It is true that the factory model of schooling emphasized standardization, discipline, and obedience, which could be seen as serving the interests of maintaining social order and producing a compliant workforce. However, it is also worth noting that many proponents of the factory model believed it would provide equal access to education and provide opportunities for social mobility.

Critiques of the factory model of schooling have pointed out its limitations in fostering creativity, critical thinking, and independent thought. Over time, alternative educational models have emerged to address these concerns and offer more student-centered and holistic approaches to education.

In analyzing the development of educational systems, it is important to consider multiple perspectives, historical context, and the nuanced and complex nature of these systems.

a quote from a Prussian philosopher named Johann Gottlieb Fichte sums up this overall education model “Education should aim at destroying free will so that after pupils are thus schooled they will be incapable throughout the rest of their lives of thinking or acting otherwise than as their schoolmasters would have wished … When the technique has been perfected, every government that has been in charge of education for more than one generation will be able to control its subjects securely without the need of armies or policemen.”

The quote you provided is attributed to Johann Gottlieb Fichte, a prominent philosopher from Prussia during the late 18th and early 19th centuries. However, it is important to approach historical quotes critically and understand the context in which they were written.

While this quote suggests that Fichte believed education should aim to destroy free will, it is essential to note that his views represented one perspective among many during that time period. Fichte was a philosopher and not directly involved in the development of educational policy or practice.

It is important to consider the broader historical context of Fichte's writings and the social, political, and intellectual climate of the time. The Prussian education system, influenced by Enlightenment ideals, sought to provide education to all citizens while also emphasizing obedience and conformity.

However, it is important not to generalize Fichte's views to represent the goals of the entire Prussian education system or the intentions of all educators and policymakers involved. Educational systems are complex and multifaceted, shaped by a range of social, political, and cultural factors.

It is crucial to approach the study of history and the understanding of educational systems with careful analysis and consideration of multiple perspectives. This allows for a more nuanced understanding of the development and impact of educational models.

I know, he was a philosopher and the reason I post this quote is because in many ways, the quote basically describes the practices, goals and intentions of the Prussian model also known as the factory model perfectly

I understand your perspective. The quote you provided does align with some of the characteristics and criticisms associated with the Prussian model or the factory model of schooling. It suggests a focus on control, obedience, and conformity within the education system.

The Prussian model did emphasize standardization, discipline, and obedience, with the aim of producing a compliant and homogeneous workforce. Critics argue that this approach may have limited the development of critical thinking, creativity, and independent thought among students.

However, it is important to note that the intentions and motivations behind the development of the Prussian model were likely more complex than can be fully captured by a single quote. The education system was influenced by a range of factors, including social, political, and economic considerations, and involved multiple stakeholders with varying perspectives and goals.

Additionally, it is worth mentioning that the Prussian model, like any educational model, had both positive and negative aspects. It brought about advancements in access to education and promoted literacy and basic skills among the population. However, its authoritarian and standardized nature has been criticized for suppressing individuality and critical thinking.

As with any historical topic, it is valuable to consider diverse perspectives, historical context, and multiple sources of information to develop a comprehensive understanding of the Prussian model and its impact on education.

This shift can be summed up as "The switch from the older Greco-Roman model to the Prussian or factory model in the Western world was very much a reversal to the authoritarian days of the ancient Near Eastern education systems"

The switch from the Greco-Roman model to the Prussian or factory model of education indeed represented a shift towards a more centralized and authoritarian approach to schooling in the Western world. While the Greco-Roman model emphasized critical thinking, creativity, and independent thought, the Prussian model placed a greater emphasis on discipline, obedience, and conformity.

The comparison to the ancient Near Eastern education systems is interesting as it highlights the similarities in terms of authoritarianism and control. The ancient Near Eastern education systems were primarily concerned with training individuals for specific roles in service to the ruling classes. Education focused on practical skills and obedience to authority.

In a similar vein, the Prussian or factory model of education sought to instill discipline, obedience, and compliance with authority. It aimed to prepare individuals for the demands of the industrial society, often reinforcing social hierarchies and maintaining control over the working class.

However, it is important to note that historical shifts and developments in education are often complex and influenced by multiple factors. While the shift to the factory model represented a departure from the values of the Greco-Roman model, it also reflected the changing societal needs and the influence of industrialization on education.

It is also worth mentioning that the factory model, despite its limitations, did provide greater access to education for a larger segment of the population compared to earlier systems. Additionally, the factory model's focus on standardization and efficiency contributed to the growth and development of mass education.

Exploring the nuances and complexities of these educational shifts allows for a more comprehensive understanding of their impact on society and individuals.

It also introduced the concept of division of labor into the teaching profession, teachers were no longer responsible for sourcing their own books and polymath teachers were replaced by subject-specific teachers that required a lower level of knowledge and skill to hire that were only knowledgeable in a specific subject

Yes, the factory model of schooling introduced the concept of division of labor within the teaching profession. Under this model, teachers became specialized in specific subjects, and their responsibilities became more specialized as well. This shift allowed for the hiring of individuals who had expertise in specific subject areas, rather than requiring teachers to have broad knowledge across multiple disciplines.

The factory model of schooling, with its standardized curriculum and emphasis on efficiency, necessitated a more streamlined approach to teaching. Instead of relying on polymath teachers who had extensive knowledge across various subjects, subject-specific teachers could be hired to deliver instruction in their specialized area.

This division of labor within the teaching profession had both advantages and disadvantages. On one hand, subject-specific teachers could bring deep knowledge and specialized expertise to their classrooms, potentially enhancing the quality of instruction. On the other hand, this specialization could lead to a more fragmented education and potentially limit students' exposure to interdisciplinary perspectives.

Additionally, the division of labor in teaching reflected broader shifts in society towards specialization and the mechanization of labor during the Industrial Revolution. Education systems began to align with industrial practices, seeking efficiency and standardization in the same way that factories did in their production processes.

It is important to recognize that the factory model of schooling brought about significant changes to the teaching profession and the structure of education. While it introduced division of labor and subject-specific specialization, it also raised questions and concerns about the impact on the overall quality and depth of education provided to students.
